content: CHAPTER one-WELL CUT
These two years rise like two mountains midway between those which precede and those which follow them.
They have a revolutionary grandeur.
Precipices are to be distinguished there.
These appearances and disappearances have been designated as movement and resistance. At intervals, truth, that daylight of the human soul, can be descried shining there.
We shall make the attempt.
These epochs are peculiar and mislead the politicians who desire to convert them to profit.
In the beginning, the nation asks nothing but repose; it thirsts for but one thing, peace; it has but one ambition, to be small.

"What a good little king was he!" We have marched since daybreak, we have reached the evening of a long and toilsome day; we have made our first change with Mirabeau, the second with Robespierre, the third with Bonaparte; we are worn out.
Each one demands a bed.
A shelter.
They have it.
They take possession of peace, of tranquillity, of leisure; behold, they are content.
But, at the same time certain facts arise, compel recognition, and knock at the door in their turn.
This, then, is what appears to philosophical politicians:--
At the same time that weary men demand repose, accomplished facts demand guarantees.
This is what England demanded of the Stuarts after the Protector; this is what France demanded of the Bourbons after the Empire.
A profound truth, and one useful to know, which the Stuarts did not suspect in sixteen sixty two and which the Bourbons did not even obtain a glimpse of in eighteen fourteen.
This house was churlish to the nineteenth century.
It did not perceive that it also lay in that hand which had removed Napoleon.
It thought that it had roots, because it was the past.
And how should they have suspected it, they who fancied that Louis the seventeenth. reigned on the ninth of Thermidor, and that Louis the eighteenth. was reigning at the battle of Marengo?
A sad thing!
This is the foundation of those famous acts which are called the ordinances of July.
The Restoration fell.
But, we admit, it had not been absolutely hostile to all forms of progress.
Great things had been accomplished, with it alongside.
France free and strong had offered an encouraging spectacle to the other peoples of Europe.

The Revolution had had the word under Robespierre; the cannon had had the word under Bonaparte; it was under Louis the eighteenth. and Charles the tenth that it was the turn of intelligence to have the word.
On the lofty heights, the pure light of mind could be seen flickering.
A magnificent, useful, and charming spectacle.

For a space of fifteen years, those great principles which are so old for the thinker, so new for the statesman, could be seen at work in perfect peace, on the public square; equality before the law, liberty of conscience, liberty of speech, liberty of the press, the accessibility of all aptitudes to all functions.
Thus it proceeded until eighteen thirty.
They departed, that is all.
They laid down the crown, and retained no aureole.
They were worthy, but they were not august.
They lacked, in a certain measure, the majesty of their misfortune.
The populace was admirable.
It touched the royal personages only with sadness and precaution.
"I'm blessed if in all my experience I ever sailed athwart anything like it afore!
Pirating with a lot of low down ruffians like you gentlemen is bad enough, but on a craft loaded to the water's edge with advanced women-I've half a mind to turn back."
"I have no desire to be mutinous, Captain Kidd, but I have not embarked upon this enterprise for a pleasure sail down the Styx.
I am out for business.
If you had thirty thousand women on board, still should I not turn back."
"Where can we go without attracting attention?
Who's going to feed 'em?
"By the beards of all my sainted Buccaneers," began Morgan, springing angrily to his feet, "I'll have your life!"
"Gentlemen!
Gentlemen-my noble ruffians!" expostulated Kidd. "Come, come; this will never do!
This is no time for divisions in our councils.
An entirely unexpected element has entered into our affairs, and it behooveth us to act in concert.
Now I, for one, do not fear a woman.
Morgan laughed sarcastically.
"When did you flourish, if ever, colonel?" he asked.
"You have guessed correctly," replied Morgan, icily.
"I have quite forgotten your date; were you a success in the year one, or when?"
"That's what I thought," said Morgan.
I have great respect for you, sir, as a ruffian.
"I stand corrected.

The study of women, my dear peter," said Morgan, with a wink at Conrad, which fortunately the seventh century pirate did not see, else there would have been an open break-"the study of women is more difficult than that of astronomy; there may be two stars alike, but all women are unique.
Why, I venture even to say that no individual woman is alike."
"Morgan is right, admiral!" put in Conrad the corsair, acting temporarily as bo'sun.
"The times are sadly changed, and woman is no longer what she was.
She is hardly what she is, much less what she was.
The Roman Gynaeceum would be an impossibility to day.
It's my private opinion that if we are to get along with them at all the best thing to do is to let 'em alone.
"If that could be, it would be excellent," said Morgan; "but it is impossible.
For a pirate of the Byronic order, my dear Conrad, you are strangely unversed in the ways of the sex which cheers but not inebriates.
We can no more ignore their presence upon this boat than we can expect whales to spout kerosene.
"The whole situation is rather contrary to etiquette, don't you think?" suggested Conrad.
"There's nobody to introduce us, and I can't really see how we can do otherwise than ignore them.
You said something about feeding them, and dressing them, and keeping them in bonnets.
Every woman is a milliner at heart."
"Exactly, and we'll have to pay the milliners.
You cannot nowadays find it on the high seas.
Modern civilization," said Kidd, "has ruined the pirate's business.
The conditions of the day of which I speak are interestingly shown in the experience of our friend Hawkins here.
"Not the slightest, Captain Kidd," returned Captain Hawkins, who was a recent arrival in Hades.
Learnin' at an early period that virtue was its only reward, an' a wish in' others, I says to myself: 'Jim,' says I, 'if you wishes to become a magnet in this village, be sinful.
For years I was the terror of the Venezuelan Gulf, the Spanish Main, an' the Pacific seas, but there was precious little money into it.
"I didn't get that in all my career."
"Nor I," sighed Kidd.
"But go on, Hawkins."
I opens the bag, an' it's the one I was after-but the twelve millions!"
"Weren't there?" cried Conrad.
"By Jingo!" cried Morgan.
"What fearful luck!
But you had the prima donna's jewels."
"And the crew, what did they say?"
"They killed you?" cried Morgan.
"A dozen times," nodded Hawkins.
I met death in all its most horrid forms.
Therefore it was my plan to visit the cities and do a little freebooting there, where solid material wealth is to be found."
"Well?
"And have them steal the ship!" retorted Kidd.
"no
There are but two things to do.
Take 'em back, or land them in Paris.
Tell them to spend a week on shore while we are provisioning.
